source: GTEx Portal (gtexportal.org) and published GTEx-derived sex-bias catalogs
sandbox_status: BLOCKED — direct GTEx Portal queries return HTTP 403 (host_not_allowed at sandbox level); upstream portal also returns 403 to WebFetch user-agent. Bash curl returns 'host_not_allowed'.
fallback_evidence_tier: secondary-literature meta-analysis level; not direct portal data
_raw_data_status: NOT EXTRACTED — sandbox-blocked. Reproduction note: the GTEx v8 median expression for ABCG2 across the 5 listed intestinal tissues is publicly viewable at https://gtexportal.org/home/gene/ABCG2 with the 'Sex' visualization toggle. This run could not reach that endpoint.

secondary_evidence_summary.source: Schärfe CPI et al. (2023) Nat Commun 14:7313 — 'Deciphering genetic causes for sex differences in human health through drug metabolism and transporter genes'
verified_facts: ABCG2 is identified as having sex-differentiated genetic effects (SDEs) in this paper
ABCG2 is implicated in gout/urate via these SDEs

Specific intestinal-tissue log2FC magnitude NOT extracted at this verification tier (paper paywalled at sandbox level; publicly available abstract + figure-caption snippets do not include the per-tissue numbers)

secondary_evidence_summary.source: Oliva M et al. (2020) Science 369:eaba3066 — 'The impact of sex on gene expression across human tissues' (GTEx v8 sex catalog)
verified_facts: GTEx v8 catalog of sex differences across 44 tissues; 37% of all genes show sex-biased expression in at least one tissue
ABCG2 specific result: NOT extracted at this verification tier (PDF behind 403 in this sandbox)
Methodology: log2FC reported per tissue with FDR < 5% threshold

secondary_evidence_summary.source: Halperin Kuhns VL & Woodward OM (2020) IJMS 21:4269 — 'Sex Differences in Urate Handling' review
verified_facts: ABCG2 had a stronger association with higher serum urate levels in males (GWAS-level)
SLC2A9 had a stronger association with lower serum urate levels in females (GWAS-level)
ER binding sites have been identified in the promoter region of ABCG2, implying ABCG2 can be transcriptionally regulated by estrogen
Mechanism framing: estrogen-positive on the female arm, NOT androgen-negative on the male arm

secondary_evidence_summary.source: MacLean C et al. (2008) DMD 36:1249-54 — full intestinal scan, male+female Wistar rats
verified_facts: Han-Wistar rats, n undisclosed at snippet level (~3-4 per group typical for this design)
Method: Western blot + qRT-PCR for P-gp (ABCB1), BCRP (ABCG2), MRP2 (ABCC2)
Tissue: duodenum, jejunum, ileum, colon — complete 3-cm-segment intestinal scan
Result: NO SIGNIFICANT SEX DIFFERENCE in BCRP/ABCG2 expression along the entire intestinal length in healthy rats
Specific fold-change-vs-zero with CI: not extracted at this verification tier; null finding qualitatively robust

secondary_evidence_summary.source: Tubic-Grozdanis M et al. (2020) Mol Pharm 17(11) — citing-paper context for MacLean 2008 + their own Wistar-rat data
verified_facts: Found ~35% higher P-glycoprotein protein in male jejunum and ileum (p<0.05) — i.e., a SEX DIFFERENCE for P-gp at modest magnitude
Did NOT report a similar effect for BCRP/ABCG2 — i.e., the null sex finding for ABCG2 in MacLean 2008 is REPLICATED in this group's Wistar work

qualitative_verdict_at_this_tier: Direct GTEx-portal numbers are NOT in this dataset due to sandbox limits. Secondary literature is consistent with: (a) ABCG2 GWAS-level sex-bias in serum urate is real (males more affected), (b) tissue-level mRNA/protein sex difference in healthy intestinal tissue is small or null (MacLean 2008 + Tubic 2020 replication), (c) the mechanism is estrogen-positive (ER binding sites in ABCG2 promoter, Halperin Kuhns 2020) rather than androgen-negative.
